title: Data Analysis in the Horsley Lab
text:
Welcome
This guide teaches reproducible, collaborative data analysis workflows for the Horsley Lab at Yale. Our lab studies skin biology, wound healing, fibrosis, and stem cells — and increasingly, our science depends on computational analysis of complex datasets: single-cell RNA-seq, spatial transcriptomics, bulk RNA-seq, and proteomics.
This handbook will help you get set up, learn the tools, and start doing real analysis — the right way from the beginning.

title: Data Analysis in the Horsley Lab
text:
Who This Guide Is For
This guide is for anyone joining the Horsley Lab who will be doing computational work. You don’t need prior experience with the tools we use — we’ll walk you through everything. If you’ve used R or Python before, great. If not, that’s fine too.

title: Data Analysis in the Horsley Lab
text:
How to Use This Book
Start with Part 1: Quick Start. Install the tools, set up your computer, and run your first analysis. Every new lab member should begin here.
Parts 2–4 are reference material you’ll return to as needed:

Part 2: Core Tools — Deeper coverage of Positron, project organization, renv, and Git/GitHub
Part 3: Working with AI — How to work effectively with Claude Code as a coding collaborator
Part 4: Single-Cell Analysis — Horsley Lab-specific workflows for scRNAseq and spatial transcriptomics
Part 5: Workflows — Practical guides for common tasks

title: Data Analysis in the Horsley Lab
text:
Our Goal
Every lab member should feel confident doing computational work — not just running scripts someone else wrote, but understanding and building their own analyses. The tools in this guide are chosen to make that easier: a modern IDE, environment managers that keep projects organized, version control for sharing code, and AI assistants that help you write, debug, and learn faster.
Everything we do is oriented around one principle: your analyses should be reproducible and understandable by others in the lab.
Questions? Email valerie.horsley@yale.edu or ask in the lab Discord.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.1 What We Analyze
Welcome to the Horsley Lab. This chapter gives you a brief overview of how we work computationally before you dive into installation and setup.
The Horsley Lab studies skin biology, wound healing, fibrosis, and stem cells. Our computational work centers on:

title: 1  Introduction
text:
Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNAseq) — profiling gene expression in individual cells to understand cell types and states in skin and wound tissue
Spatial transcriptomics (Visium) — measuring gene expression while preserving tissue spatial context
Bulk RNA sequencing — comparing gene expression across conditions or genotypes
Proteomics and lipidomics — measuring protein and lipid profiles in tissues and extracellular vesicles

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.2 Our Tools
We use a standard set of tools for all computational work in the lab:



Tool
What it’s for




R
Statistical analysis, scRNAseq, plotting


Seurat
Single-cell RNA-seq analysis


Positron
Our IDE (coding environment)


renv
R package management


Git & GitHub
Version control and collaboration


Claude Code
AI coding assistant


Quarto
Writing analysis documents



You don’t need to know any of these yet — the next chapter will walk you through installing everything.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.3 How We Organize Work
Every analysis project in the lab follows a standard folder structure:
project-name/
├── data/
│   ├── raw/          ← original files, never modified
│   └── processed/    ← cleaned/transformed data
├── scripts/
│   ├── 01_qc.Rmd          ← numbered in order of execution
│   ├── 02_preprocessing.Rmd
│   └── scratch/           ← exploratory files
├── outputs/
│   ├── figures/
│   └── tables/
├── docs/
└── README.md
This structure makes it easy for anyone in the lab to understand and reproduce your work. You’ll set this up automatically using our /new-project skill in Chapter 3.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.4 Working with AI
We use Claude Code as an AI coding assistant throughout our work. It can help you write code, debug errors, understand scripts, and audit your analyses. We have custom lab skills that teach Claude our conventions — you’ll install these in the next chapter.






NoteA Note on AI and Learning



Claude Code is a powerful tool, but it’s not a substitute for understanding your analysis. Use it to help you write and debug code, but always make sure you understand what the code is doing and why. The /learn-code skill exists specifically to help you build that understanding.


Let’s get started with installation.

title: 2  Installation
text:
2.1 Step 1: Find Out Your Mac Chip
This chapter walks you through setting up everything you need for computational work in the Horsley Lab. Do these steps in order.
You need to know whether your Mac has an Apple Silicon chip (M1/M2/M3/M4) or an Intel chip, because you’ll download different software versions for each.
Open Terminal (search for it in Spotlight with Cmd+Space, type “Terminal”) and run:
Write this down — you’ll need it for the next steps.

title: 2  Installation
text:
uname -m

arm64 → Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3/M4)
x86_64 → Intel

title: 2  Installation
text:
2.2 Step 2: Install R
We use R for all statistical analysis and single-cell work.

Go to cloud.r-project.org
Click Download R for macOS
Download the version matching your chip (Apple Silicon or Intel)
Open the .pkg file and follow the installer

To verify it worked, open Terminal and run:
R --version
You should see something like R version 4.4.x.

title: 2  Installation
text:
2.3 Step 3: Install Positron
Positron is our coding environment (IDE). It’s built for R and Python and has Claude Code built in.

Go to positron.posit.co
Download the version matching your chip
Open the .dmg file and drag Positron to your Applications folder
Open Positron from your Applications folder

title: 2  Installation
text:
2.4 Step 4: Install Quarto
Quarto is what we use to write analysis documents that combine code, output, and text.

Go to quarto.org/docs/get-started
Download the macOS installer
Run the installer

title: 2  Installation
text:
2.5 Step 5: Install Claude Code
Claude Code is the AI coding assistant we use in the lab.
Follow the install guide at: docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview






Important



You’ll need an Anthropic account to use Claude Code. Ask Valerie if you need help getting access through the lab account.

title: 2  Installation
text:
2.6 Step 6: Install the Lab Skills
Our lab has custom Claude Code skills that teach Claude our conventions. These must be installed in the Mac Terminal (not the Positron sidebar).
Open Terminal, type claude to launch Claude Code, then run:
/plugin marketplace add https://github.com/Horsleylab/horsley-lab-skills
/plugin install horsley-lab-skills






Tip



If /plugin gives you an error, make sure you typed claude first to launch Claude Code before running the plugin commands.


To verify the skills installed, run in Terminal:
ls ~/.claude/skills/
You should see folders including audit-script, learn-code, new-project, and others.

title: 2  Installation
text:
2.7 Step 7: Install Git
Git is how we track changes to code and collaborate with others.
Check if you already have it:
git --version
If not installed, macOS will prompt you to install it automatically. Follow the prompts.
Then configure it with your name and email:
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your.email@yale.edu"

title: 2  Installation
text:
2.8 Step 8: Create a GitHub Account
If you don’t have one, create a free account at github.com. Use your Yale email address.
Then ask Valerie to add you to the Horsleylab GitHub organization.

title: 2  Installation
text:
2.9 You’re Ready
Once all steps are complete, move on to Chapter 3 where you’ll create your first analysis project.






NoteGetting Help



If you get stuck at any step, ask in the lab Discord #resources channel or email valerie.horsley@yale.edu.

title: 3  Your First R Project
text:
3.1 Open Positron
In this chapter you’ll create your first analysis project using the Horsley Lab conventions. We’ll use the /new-project skill to set everything up automatically.
Open Positron from your Applications folder. You’ll see a layout similar to RStudio but more modern:

title: 3  Your First R Project
text:
Left sidebar — file explorer, git, extensions
Main area — code editor
Bottom panel — terminal, console, output
Right sidebar — Claude Code chat panel

title: 3  Your First R Project
text:
3.2 Create a Project Folder
First, create a folder for your project. In Terminal:
mkdir ~/projects/my-first-project
cd ~/projects/my-first-project
Then in Positron, go to File → Open Folder and open my-first-project.

title: 3  Your First R Project
section: 3.3 Use /new-project to Set Up the Structure
text:
3.3 Use /new-project to Set Up the Structure
In the Claude Code sidebar in Positron, type:
/new-project
Claude will ask you a few questions:

Project type — choose Data science
Project name — my-first-project (or whatever you chose)
Brief description — one sentence about what this project is for
Python needed? — No for now
Layout — Flat
Cluster? — No for now
GitHub — Personal account, Private

Claude Code will then create the full folder structure, initialize renv, create a README, CLAUDE.md, and set up git.






Tip



The /new-project skill sets up everything according to Horsley Lab conventions automatically. You don’t need to create folders manually.

title: 3  Your First R Project
text:
3.4 Explore the Structure
After /new-project finishes, you should see:
my-first-project/
├── data/
│   ├── raw/
│   └── processed/
├── scripts/
│   └── scratch/
├── outputs/
│   ├── figures/
│   └── tables/
├── docs/
├── .claude/
│   └── CLAUDE.md
├── renv/
├── renv.lock
└── README.md

title: 3  Your First R Project
text:
3.5 Create Your First Script
In Positron, create a new file called 01_import.Rmd in the scripts/ folder.
Add this content:
---
title: "01 — Import and Explore Data"
date: "2026-04-21"
output: html_document
---



## Load Data


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# We'll add data loading here
cat("Script is working!\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Script is working!
```


:::
:::

title: 3  Your First R Project
text:
3.6 Run the Script
Click the Run button next to any code chunk, or use Cmd+Shift+Enter to run the current chunk. You should see “Script is working!” in the console.

title: 3  Your First R Project
text:
3.7 Use /done to Wrap Up
At the end of any working session, type in the Claude Code sidebar:
/done
This will summarize what you did, update your CLAUDE.md session log, and commit your work to git.

title: 3  Your First R Project
text:
3.8 Next Steps
You now have a working project with the correct Horsley Lab structure. In the next chapter, you’ll learn how to work with Claude Code day-to-day to help you write and understand code.

title: 4  Working with Claude Code
text:
4.1 What Claude Code Can Do
Claude Code is an AI coding assistant built into Positron. This chapter covers how to use it effectively for day-to-day analysis work in the lab.
Claude Code can help you:

title: 4  Working with Claude Code
text:
Write code — describe what you want and it writes the R or Python
Debug errors — paste an error message and it explains what went wrong and how to fix it
Understand code — use /learn-code to walk through any script section by section
Audit scripts — use /audit-script before submitting work for review
Set up projects — use /new-project to scaffold a new analysis
Wrap up sessions — use /done to commit work and update your session log

title: 4  Working with Claude Code
text:
4.2 The Lab Skills
When you installed the Horsley Lab skills, you gave Claude Code knowledge of our specific conventions. This means Claude already knows:

Our folder structure (data/raw/, scripts/scratch/, outputs/figures/)
Seurat-specific best practices (checking active assay, tracking cell counts, etc.)
How to audit an R script for common single-cell analysis mistakes
How to set up a new project the Horsley Lab way

title: 4  Working with Claude Code
section: 4.3 How to Ask for Help Effectively
text:
4.3 How to Ask for Help Effectively
The more context you give Claude Code, the better it can help. Instead of:

“My code isn’t working”

Try:

“I’m trying to run FindMarkers() on my Seurat object but getting an error that says ‘object of type closure is not subsettable’. Here’s my code: [paste code]”

Good things to include: - The exact error message - The relevant code - What you were trying to do - What data you’re working with (e.g., “a Seurat object with 8,000 cells from wound healing tissue”)

title: 4  Working with Claude Code
text:
4.4 The Slash Commands
Type these in the Claude Code sidebar:



Command
When to use it




/new-project
Starting a new analysis project


/new-plan
Planning a multi-session analysis


/learn-code
Understanding a script you didn’t write


/audit-script
Before sending a script for review


/done
At the end of every working session

title: 4  Working with Claude Code
text:
4.5 A Typical Day
Here’s what a typical working session looks like:

Open your project in Positron (File → Open Folder)
Check the session log in .claude/CLAUDE.md to see where you left off
Work on your analysis — ask Claude Code for help as needed
Run /done at the end of the session to commit and update the log

title: 4  Working with Claude Code
section: 4.6 Before You Send Code for Review
text:
4.6 Before You Send Code for Review
Before sending any script to Valerie or a collaborator for review, always run:
/audit-script
This catches bugs, analytical issues, and reproducibility problems before anyone else sees them. It will produce a report in outputs/audit_reports/.






ImportantRunning /audit-script before review is lab policy



Think of it like spell-checking before sending an email — it’s a quick step that catches problems and shows you’ve thought carefully about your work.

title: 4  Working with Claude Code
text:
4.7 Getting Help
If Claude Code can’t help with something, ask in the lab Discord or email valerie.horsley@yale.edu.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.1 Why Positron?
In Chapter 3, you used Positron to set up a project, write code in the editor, and run it in the console. This chapter digs deeper into how Positron works and how to use it effectively for daily analysis work. We’ll cover what each panel does, how to navigate your project, and habits that make working smoother.
Positron is our IDE (Integrated Development Environment) — it’s where you write code, explore data, run analyses, and manage projects. Built by Posit (the same team behind RStudio), Positron keeps the best RStudio-style data science features — the plotting pane, environment viewer, data viewer — while adding the best parts of VS Code, including Git integration and Claude Code.
If you’ve used RStudio before, Positron will feel familiar. You still get an R console, a variable explorer, a data viewer, and a file browser. A few features that make it particularly useful for our work:
Full support for R and Python. Most of our work is in R (Seurat, DESeq2), but some tools require Python. In Positron, you can run R and Python sessions side by side, each with its own console and environment.
An excellent Data Viewer. Click any dataframe in the Variables pane and it opens in an interactive viewer where you can sort, filter, and search. For Seurat metadata tables, this is invaluable.
Claude Code integration. The Claude Code sidebar is built directly into Positron — you can ask for help, run lab skills, and get code reviewed right alongside your scripts.

title: 5  Positron
section: 5.2 The Bottom Panel: Console, Terminal, and Variables
text:
5.2 The Bottom Panel: Console, Terminal, and Variables
The bottom of the Positron window is where most interactive work happens. It contains tabs for your R console and terminal.

5.2.1 Console vs Terminal
These are the two most important tabs, and they do fundamentally different things.
The Console is your R session. When you press Cmd+Enter (Mac) or Ctrl+Enter (Windows) to run a code chunk, it executes here. This is where you see R output, error messages, and package loading messages.
The Terminal is a system shell — the same as Terminal.app on macOS or PowerShell on Windows, but built into Positron. Use it for shell commands: running git, rendering Quarto documents, or anything you’d normally do at the command line.
The key distinction: R code goes in the Console, shell commands go in the Terminal. If Claude Code tells you to run git add ., that goes in the Terminal. If it tells you to run library(Seurat), that goes in the Console.






Tip



Mixing these up is a common early mistake. You’ll get object not found if you type a shell command into the R console, or command not found if you type R code into the terminal.








NoteWindows Users (PowerShell terminal)



On Windows, Positron’s integrated terminal is PowerShell, not bash/zsh. Basic navigation commands work the same (cd, ls, mkdir), but some Unix-style commands used in this guide may differ. Key differences:

Directory listing: ls works but behaves slightly differently than on Mac
File paths: Use backslashes (\) in PowerShell, or forward slashes (/) which also work in most contexts
Conda activation: If conda activate doesn’t work, run conda init powershell in PowerShell first, then restart

When in doubt, commands shown in this book work on both platforms unless otherwise noted.




5.2.2 Managing Multiple Sessions
As you work, the bottom panel accumulates tabs — you might have an R console and one or two terminals open. Each appears as a labeled tab. Click a tab to switch to that session.
Be careful with multiple R sessions: whichever console tab is visible is where Cmd+Enter (or Ctrl+Enter) sends your code. If you have two R sessions open and you’re looking at the wrong one, your code runs in a session that might not have the objects you expect.


5.2.3 The Variables Pane
The Variables pane shows every object in your current R session — Seurat objects with their dimensions, dataframes with row and column counts, vectors, lists. Click any dataframe to open it in the Data Viewer. The pane updates in real time as you create, modify, or remove objects.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.3 The Data Viewer
The Data Viewer is one of Positron’s most useful features for data analysis work. There are two ways to open it:

Click any dataframe in the Variables pane
Click a .csv or .tsv file in the File Explorer — Positron opens it directly in the viewer

Once open, the Data Viewer lets you:

Sort by clicking column headers
Filter rows using the filter bar
Search for specific values across the table
See distributions via column histograms at the top of each numeric column







TipSeurat Objects



You can’t view a Seurat object directly in the Data Viewer — it’s a complex nested structure. To inspect cell metadata, run View(seu@meta.data) in the console. This opens just the metadata table in the viewer.


During exploratory analysis, keep the Data Viewer open constantly — check data after each transformation, look for unexpected values, verify that joins worked correctly.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.4 Navigating Your Project

5.4.1 The File Explorer and Outline
The File Explorer in the left sidebar shows all files and folders in your project folder. Click files to open them — .csv and .tsv files open in the Data Viewer; code files open in the editor. Right-click to create, rename, or delete files.
The Outline panel shows the structure of the current document — for .Rmd files, it lists headings and code chunks. Click any item to jump to that section. Once your scripts get longer, the Outline is essential for navigation.


5.4.2 Opening Files Quickly
Press Cmd+P (Mac) or Ctrl+P (Windows) to open the Quick Open bar — start typing part of a filename and Positron shows matching files. Much faster than clicking through the File Explorer once your project has many files.


5.4.3 Working with Multiple Files
Files open as tabs in the editor. Drag a tab to the right edge to split the view and see two files side by side — useful when reading one script’s outputs while writing code that depends on them.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.5 Search
Press Cmd+Shift+F (Mac) or Ctrl+Shift+F (Windows) to search across every file in your project. Useful for finding where a function is defined, locating every use of a variable, or searching for a specific column name across all your scripts.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.6 The Command Palette
The Command Palette is Positron’s universal command search. Open it with Cmd+Shift+P (Mac) or Ctrl+Shift+P (Windows), then start typing what you want to do. It searches through every available command — switching R versions, changing settings, toggling panels.
You’ll encounter it most often when Claude Code gives you instructions like “open the Command Palette and type ‘R: Select R Binary’.” Rather than memorizing where every setting lives in menus, you type what you’re looking for.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.7 The Status Bar
The status bar runs along the very bottom of the Positron window. It shows:

Active R version (e.g., “R 4.4.2”) — click to change
Current Git branch (e.g., “main”)
Line and column number for your cursor position

When something isn’t working — packages seem missing, or code behaves unexpectedly — check the status bar first. It often reveals the problem: you’re running the wrong R version.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.8 Essential Keyboard Shortcuts



Action
Mac
Windows




Run current line/selection
Cmd+Enter
Ctrl+Enter


Run current chunk
Cmd+Shift+Enter
Ctrl+Shift+Enter


Open Command Palette
Cmd+Shift+P
Ctrl+Shift+P


Quick file open
Cmd+P
Ctrl+P


Toggle terminal
Cmd+`
Ctrl+`


Save file
Cmd+S
Ctrl+S


Project-wide search
Cmd+Shift+F
Ctrl+Shift+F



Cmd+Enter (or Ctrl+Enter) is the one you’ll use constantly. The Command Palette is how you access anything in Positron you’re not sure how to find.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.9 Working with R

5.9.1 Starting and Restarting R
An R session starts automatically when you run R code from an .Rmd file. If the project has renv set up, you’ll see a message like Project loaded. [renv 1.0.0] confirming project-specific packages are active.
Restarting R gives you a clean slate and is something you should do regularly. Click the restart icon (↻) in the Console toolbar, or press Cmd+Shift+0 (Mac) / Ctrl+Shift+0 (Windows).
Why restart? During interactive development, your R session accumulates objects — some created by your script, some typed manually in the console while exploring. Over time it gets hard to tell what’s in your script versus what you created on the fly. Restarting clears everything and lets you test whether your script is truly self-contained.
After restarting, run your script from the top. If it executes cleanly, your script has all the code it needs. If it fails with “object not found” errors, something is missing from the script.






WarningWorks interactively but fails on render?



This is a very common problem. You run chunks one by one and everything works, but when you render the document it fails. The cause is almost always an object you created manually in the console that isn’t in any chunk.
To diagnose: restart R, then run all chunks from the top. Whatever fails is missing from your script.




5.9.2 Setting the R Version
If you need to change which R version Positron uses:

Open the Command Palette (Cmd+Shift+P / Ctrl+Shift+P)
Type “R: Select R Binary”
Choose the version you want

This only needs to be done once per project — Positron remembers your choice.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.10 Source Control Basics
The Source Control panel (click the branch icon in the left sidebar, or Cmd+Shift+G / Ctrl+Shift+G) shows which files have changed since your last commit.
Basic workflow: 1. Make changes to your files 2. Open Source Control — review changed files 3. Stage files by clicking the + icon 4. Type a commit message 5. Click the checkmark to commit 6. Push via the three-dot menu (⋯) → Push






Note/done handles commits



Once you’re comfortable with the basics, the /done skill in Claude Code handles committing your work at the end of each session. But understanding the Source Control panel helps you verify that commits are correct.


See ?sec-git for a full introduction to Git and GitHub.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.11 When Things Go Wrong

5.11.1 A Panel Disappeared
Go to the View menu and look for the panel you need — View → Terminal, View → Console, etc. Or use the Command Palette and type the panel name.


5.11.2 Console Won’t Start
Check the Output panel (View → Output) and select “R” from the dropdown — this shows diagnostic messages that often explain the actual error. Also check the status bar to verify the R path is correct. If all else fails, restart Positron.


5.11.3 Wrong R Version Running
Check the status bar at the bottom. If it’s wrong, use the Command Palette: “R: Select R Binary”.


5.11.4 Everything Feels Broken
Close Positron completely and reopen your project. This fixes most transient issues — stuck processes, stale caches, panels that won’t respond.
On Windows, if Positron hangs on close, use Task Manager to end the process before reopening.

title: 5  Positron
text:
5.12 Extensions
Positron comes with most of what you need built in. A few extensions worth adding:

Quarto — for .qmd file support (may already be installed)
Jupyter — for Python notebook support
Claude Code — the AI coding assistant, if not already installed

Install extensions through the Extensions panel (Cmd+Shift+X / Ctrl+Shift+X), search for the name, and click Install.






TipStart Minimal



Don’t install a dozen extensions on day one. Start with the essentials above and add more only when you encounter a specific need.
